name: Old Simpson Quarry
short_description: Historic quarry in central Connecticut offering access to pegmatite minerals including feldspar, mica, and quartz. A moderate difficulty site ideal for collectors interested in granitic mineral spe...
description:
Old Simpson Quarry sits within Connecticut's crystalline bedrock belt, where Proterozoic and Paleozoic metamorphic rocks form the foundation of the state's geology. The quarry exposes coarse-grained pegmatitic granite, a product of late-stage cooling of igneous intrusions that allowed large mineral crystals to develop. Feldspar dominates here, often appearing in creamy-white to light pink varieties, accompanied by silvery mica sheets and translucent quartz pieces.

The site has historical significance as a working stone quarry, offering rockhounds a chance to observe natural mineral associations in an already-exposed rock face. Visitors will find quartz crystals lining joint planes, well-formed feldspar books, and flaky biotite mica scattered throughout the pit. The quarry walls reveal the granitic composition clearly, making it an excellent teaching location for understanding pegmatite formation.

Access and conditions vary seasonally. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable collecting weather in Connecticut, with moderate temperatures and lower humidity. The quarry terrain includes exposed rock faces, loose talus slopes, and uneven ground typical of worked stone pits. Visitors should approach cautiously and respect any posted safety notices. The surrounding area includes residential neighborhoods, so noise and collection hours may be restricted.

This location appeals to mineral collectors seeking pegmatite specimens, educators demonstrating granitic mineral associations, and anyone interested in Connecticut's natural stone heritage. Findings include matrix specimens combining multiple mineral types, ideal for reference collections or educational displays.
history: Old Simpson Quarry exposes Connecticut's metamorphic basement rock suite, formed during the Alleghenian orogeny when continental collisions created high-grade metamorphic conditions across New England. Subsequent granitic intrusions, including pegmatitic phases, cooled slowly to produce the large feldspar crystals and mica books visible today. The quarry itself represents 19th and 20th century stone extraction operations that created the accessible mineral exposures rockhounders find now.
tips: Focus your search on the quarry walls and exposed joint planes where quartz crystals project outward and feldspar pockets are most visible. Use a small chisel or flat pry bar to carefully extract matrix specimens rather than loose individual crystals - the mineral associations are more valuable. Look for areas where mica sheets peel naturally from the rock face, and check the quarry floor for weathered feldspar fragments. Morning visits are best for photography and detailed specimen work before afternoon heat and shadows obscure fine details.
terrain_notes: The quarry occupies a roughly 200-acre worked pit with exposed granite walls ranging from 20 to 50 feet high. Talus slopes of loose rock fragments and quarry spoil surround the main pit. Elevation varies from approximately 100 to 250 feet above surrounding terrain. Vegetation is sparse on active quarry floors but thicker on perimeter slopes. Walking distance from parking to active collecting areas ranges from 100 yards to half a mile depending on access points.
best_season: April - November, with peak conditions May - June and September - October when temperatures are mild and humidity is lowest. Summer heat can be intense in the open pit; winter snow and ice create safety hazards on rock faces and talus slopes.
written_directions: From Glastonbury center, take CT-2A north for approximately 2.5 miles. Turn east on Old Stone Hill Road and proceed 1.2 miles. The quarry access is marked by stone walls and informal parking pullouts along the road. Approach cautiously as this is an active or recently active quarry with private ownership adjacent areas. Verify current access conditions with local authorities before visiting.
road_conditions: Old Stone Hill Road is a paved state road with moderate traffic. Parking pullouts are unpaved gravel shoulders suitable for 2-3 vehicles. No high-clearance vehicle required.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along Old Stone Hill Road with capacity for 2-3 vehicles. No designated parking area. Parking should not obstruct traffic or private driveways.
collection_rules: Personal collection of small mineral specimens for educational and hobby purposes only. No commercial extraction permitted. Respect all posted signage and barrier fencing. Do not remove large pieces or collect more than can reasonably be carried. Connecticut state law prohibits damage to natural or cultural resources on public land - leave no permanent marks or enlarged holes. The quarry may have active operations - observe all safety barriers and do not enter restricted zones.
rules:
Personal use only
No commercial collection
Respect posted signage and barriers
Collect only small portable specimens
Do not damage quarry infrastructure
Follow all Connecticut collecting regulations
Observe active quarry safety protocols
nearest_services: Glastonbury town center, approximately 2.5 miles south, offers gas stations, restaurants, and shops. No services immediately adjacent to quarry.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with loose talus slopes and exposed quarry walls. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. No wheelchair access. Some sections involve scrambling over rock rubble. Quarry walls present falling rock hazards - do not stand directly beneath exposed faces. Limited shade in the open pit.

faq.question: What minerals can I find at Old Simpson Quarry in Connecticut?
faq.answer: Old Simpson Quarry is known for pegmatite minerals including cream to pink feldspar, silvery biotite mica, and clear to milky quartz. Specimens often occur as matrix pieces with multiple minerals together, making them valuable for collections. The coarse-grained pegmatitic granite here produces well-formed crystal faces and mica books.

faq.question: Is there a fee to rockhound at Old Simpson Quarry near Glastonbury?
faq.answer: No fee is required to collect at Old Simpson Quarry. It is a free public access site where personal mineral collection is permitted. You do not need a permit, but you should respect posted signage and follow Connecticut collecting regulations.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit Old Simpson Quarry for rockhounding?
faq.answer: May through October offers the best collecting conditions, with peak comfort in May - June and September - October. Summer heat can be intense in the open quarry pit, while winter snow and ice create hazards on rock faces and talus slopes. Spring and fall provide mild temperatures ideal for extended collecting sessions.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect feldspar and mica at this Connecticut quarry?
faq.answer: A small cold chisel, flat pry bar, or old screwdriver works well for extracting matrix specimens from quarry walls and joint planes. A small hammer for striking, heavy gloves for protection from sharp rock edges, and a sturdy backpack for carrying specimens are essential. Avoid motorized tools as they are not permitted.

faq.question: Is Old Simpson Quarry in Glastonbury suitable for beginner rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Old Simpson Quarry is beginner-friendly for ages 8 and up. The minerals are visible and accessible on exposed quarry walls, and no special collecting skills are required. However, families should supervise children carefully due to uneven terrain, loose rock, and quarry wall hazards. Proper footwear is essential.

faq.question: How do I get to Old Simpson Quarry from Glastonbury, and is it easy to find?
faq.answer: From Glastonbury center, take CT-2A north for 2.5 miles, then turn east on Old Stone Hill Road for 1.2 miles. Look for stone walls and informal gravel pullouts that mark the quarry access. Parking is limited to 2-3 vehicles in unpaved shoulder areas. Verify current access conditions with local authorities before visiting, as quarry operations may affect access.

meta_description: Find feldspar, mica, and quartz at Old Simpson Quarry near Glastonbury. Free public access pegmatite site ideal for beginner rockhounders. No fee required.
